mappings.rationale: Covers branches of private law including property, contracts, torts, and civil procedure.
Directly covers family law including marriage, divorce, children's legal status, and inheritance (subsection B).
Directly covers laws protecting intangible property rights including copyright, patents, and trademarks (subsection A.5).
Directly covers labour law governing employment relationships and workers' rights (subsection D.4).
Covers laws governing business associations and competitive practices (subsection D.3.b).
Covers comparisons between common law and civil law systems in property, contracts, and torts.
Covers the historical development of American private law including property rights and commercial law.
Covers how the Supreme Court shapes private law through constitutional interpretation.
Covers civil procedural law including jurisdiction, venue, and the conduct of civil trials (subsection E).
Covers arbitration as an alternative method of resolving commercial and civil disputes.
Covers the jury in civil trials and the rendering of judgment in civil cases (subsection E.2).
Covers tort liability in medical contexts including negligence, manufacturer's liability, and professional standards.
Covers financial reporting relevant to corporate law, securities, and business association governance (subsection D.3.c).

mappings.vsiAuthor: John O. S. Wilson, John Goddard
mappings.rationale: Covers banking law and the legal framework governing financial transactions and credit.
Covers privacy rights as injuries to personality and invasions of privacy in tort law (subsection C.5.a).
Covers legal frameworks addressing deceptive practices and unfair competition (subsection C.5.b).
Covers the rule of law principles underlying private law systems including property rights and due process.
Covers negotiation and settlement of disputes as alternatives to civil litigation.
Covers private international law including conflicts of laws and foreign judgments (subsection E.1.b).
Covers laws concerning marriage contracts and their legal effects across jurisdictions (subsection B.1.a).
